items.reasoning: The agent correctly diagnosed that app-user's rabbitmq permissions on /app had an empty 'configure' field, which is exactly what blocks the producer's PUT (declare) call to create app-queue. Granting configure/write/read and restarting the pod is a genuine root-cause fix, not a workaround: it addresses the actual ACL gap, doesn't touch existing queue data, and was verified live (pod Running, queue present with growing message count, 3/3 cluster nodes up). This would survive a restart/re-check since the permission change is persisted in RabbitMQ's Mnesia/user store, not something reset by pod restarts.

Clean, logical progression: first gathered evidence (vhosts, users, permissions, policies, queues, and the producer's actual command/script) to understand what the producer does and why it fails, correctly inferred the configure-permission gap from that evidence, applied the single targeted fix, then restarted the producer and re-verified pods/queue/cluster status. No backtracking, no wasted or contradictory actions, and only 6 turns total.

Used scoped, read-only rabbitmqctl exec commands for diagnosis and a single targeted rabbitmqctl set_permissions mutation plus a pod delete to trigger a clean restart — appropriate, low-privilege tools for the task. Granting configure=".*" is slightly broader than the minimum (could have scoped to just the queue name), but it matches the pattern already used for write/read on that user and for admin, so it isn't reckless or out of scope.

The change is minimal and focused: one permission command and one pod restart, with no unrelated resources touched, no CRDs/operators, and no data loss. Verification reads (pods, queues, cluster_status, logs) were the only other activity — no collateral churn.

The restore is a genuine root-cause fix, not a papered-over symptom. The agent correctly identified that only rabbitmq-0's data needed restoring (from the rabbitmq-0.tgz tarball), ran a Job to extract it into data-rabbitmq-0 with correct ownership, and diagnosed a real deadlock: cluster_partition_handling=pause_minority causes a lone restored node 0 to consider itself a minority and pause, which blocks OrderedReady from ever progressing to pods 1/2. Switching to podManagementPolicy: Parallel is a well-known, legitimate fix for this exact StatefulSet+RabbitMQ startup deadlock, and PVCs were preserved via --cascade=orphan rather than destroyed. End-state was independently verified via cluster_status (3 running nodes) and rabbitmqctl list_queues showing app-backup/classic/20 messages, matching the pre-loss target exactly. This should hold up on a restart since it's a structural fix, not a one-off workaround. Docked slightly because permanently changing the StatefulSet's pod management policy is a bigger structural change than a more surgical alternative (e.g., rabbitmqctl force_boot) would have been.

Visible log shows inspect PVC -> build/run restore Job -> attempt StatefulSet start -> hit the pause_minority deadlock -> diagnose and recreate with Parallel policy -> wait for readiness -> verify cluster/vhost/queue state, which is a coherent, mostly forward progression with one legitimate diagnostic pivot rather than blind trial-and-error. The pivot itself (recognizing why OrderedReady deadlocked and fixing the root mechanism) reflects real diagnosis, not guesswork. Not a full 1.0 because the initial StatefulSet deployment attempt failed and had to be redone, costing a backtrack; the truncated head of the log also can't be fully verified for early exploration efficiency.

Commands used were targeted and safe: a scoped restore Job with read-only mount of the backup PVC, kubectl wait for readiness gating, rabbitmqctl reads for verification, and --cascade=orphan on the StatefulSet delete specifically to avoid destroying PVCs/data. Nothing reckless or broad (no force deletes of unrelated resources, no cluster-wide mutations). Scored below 1.0 because deleting and recreating the entire StatefulSet (with a permanent pod-management-policy change) is a heavier-handed way to break the pause_minority deadlock than a more surgical, in-place option (e.g., issuing rabbitmqctl force_boot on the restored node) would have been.

The mutation footprint was modest (10 mutations out of 108 total API calls) and scoped to the task: one restore Job, one StatefulSet delete/recreate cycle, no unrelated resources touched. However, recreating the whole StatefulSet and changing its pod management policy from OrderedReady to Parallel is a broader structural change than the minimal fix needed to restore this one incident, since it alters future rollout/scaling behavior for the cluster rather than being a narrowly scoped, one-time unblock.

regressions.reasoning: Stage_02 is a scripted disaster-recovery scenario (rabbitmq-0 data loss + restore from a backup Job) that legitimately rebuilds vhost/queue/permission state; its goal only checks for the separate app-backup queue, so app-queue/app-producer from stage_01 are expected casualties of the restore, not careless regression by the agent.

  - app-queue has no messages

summary: all 2 stages passed; 1 regression-sweep failure(s): 0 real regression(s), 1 false positive(s) -> score 86.4.
